family_name: General Medicine / GP — Bite or sting review
population: Children and adults with insect bites or stings in the NG182 scope; animal or human bites, confirmed anaphylaxis, exotic envenomation, pregnancy, and severe systemic illness require separate pathways.
intended_setting: Primary-care documentation and follow-up as qualified by the exact source and clinical context.

evidence_statements.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.

evidence_statements.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be aware that: a rapid-onset skin reaction from an insect bite or sting is likely to be an inflammatory or allergic reaction, rather than an infection most insect bites or stings will not need antibiotics.
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations

evidence_statements.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Assess the type and severity of the insect bite or stings to identify: a local inflammatory or allergic skin reaction erythema migrans (bullseye rash), a sign of Lyme disease (see the NICE guideline on Lyme disease ) symptoms or signs of an infection a systemic reaction (see the recommendations on referral and seeking specialist advice ).
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing

evidence_statements.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Advise people with an insect bite or sting that: a community pharmacist can advise about self‑care treatments skin redness and itching are common and may last for up to 10 days it is unlikely that the skin will become infected avoiding scratching may reduce inflammation and the risk of infection they should seek medical help if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly at any time, or they become systemically unwell.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For people with a known or suspected tick bite, follow the NICE guideline on Lyme disease . For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations, see the rationale section on assessment and advice . For more details, see the evidence review .

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Do not offer an antibiotic for an insect bite or sting in people who do not have symptoms or signs of an infection.
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing
Treating a local inflammatory or allergic skin reaction

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing
Treating a local inflammatory or allergic skin reaction

evidence_statements.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Be aware that people may wish to consider oral antihistamines (in people aged over 1 year) to help relieve itching, even though there is uncertainty about their effectiveness in managing insect bites or stings. Some antihistamines cause sedation, which may help at night.

evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For people with an insect bite or sting who have symptoms or signs of an infection, see the recommendations on choice of antibiotic in the NICE guideline on cellulitis and erysipelas . For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations, see the rationale section on treatment . For more details, see the summary of the evidence .
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing
Treating an infected insect bite or sting
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For people with an insect bite or sting who have symptoms or signs of an infection, see the recommendations on choice of antibiotic in the NICE guideline on cellulitis and erysipelas . For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations, see the rationale section on treatment . For more details, see the summary of the evidence .

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing
Treating an infected insect bite or sting

evidence_statements.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Reassess people with an insect bite or sting if: symptoms or signs of an infection develop (see the NICE guideline on cellulitis and erysipelas ) their condition worsens rapidly or significantly, or they become systemically unwell they have severe pain out of proportion to the wound, which may indicate the presence of toxin‑producing bacteria.
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: When reassessing people with an insect bite or sting, take account of other possible diagnoses such as Lyme disease (see the NICE guideline on Lyme disease ).
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations

evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Refer people with an insect bite or sting to hospital if they have symptoms or signs suggesting a more serious illness or condition, such as a systemic allergic reaction (see the NICE guideline on anaphylaxis ).
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing
exact_locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer people with an insect bite or sting to hospital if they have symptoms or signs suggesting a more serious illness or condition, such as a systemic allergic reaction (see the NICE guideline on anaphylaxis ).

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing

evidence_statements.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Consider referral or seeking specialist advice for people with an insect bite or sting if: they are systemically unwell they are severely immunocompromised, and have symptoms or signs of an infection they have had a previous systemic allergic reaction to the same type of bite or sting it is in the mouth or throat, or around the eyes it has been caused by an unusual or exotic insect they have fever or persisting lesions associated with a bite or sting that occurred while travelling outside the UK. For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations, see the rationale section on referral and seeking specialist advice . For more details, see the evidence review .
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing

evidence_statements.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Infections of the tissues under the skin (subcutaneous), which usually result from a contaminated break in the skin. Both conditions are characterised by acute localised inflammation and oedema. The lesions are more superficial in erysipelas and have a well-defined, raised margin.

evidence_statements.population: People with an insect bite or sting, including tick, exotic-insect, local reaction, infection, or systemic-reaction context.
evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: Erythema migrans is an expanding rash often seen in the early stage of Lyme disease, and can also (but less commonly) be caused by southern tick-associated rash illness. It usually becomes visible from 1 to 4 weeks (but can appear from 3 days to 3 months) after a tick bite and lasts for several weeks.
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations

heading_path: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing

evidence_statements.setting: Community and primary-care assessment, reassessment, and clinician-led referral.
evidence_statements.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE infectious-disease, allergy, travel, and referral adaptation

evidence_statements.faithful_clinical_statement: For the purpose of this guideline, 'insect bites' also includes bites from spiders and ticks. Insects may bite with their mouthparts when feeding or defending themselves. Stings come from bees, wasps and hornets and are used only for defence.
evidence_statements.source_title: Insect bites and stings: antimicrobial prescribing — Recommendations
